subtitle: How the Sword-Bearing Nayakas Carried, Shared, and Remade the Power of Vijayanagara

panels.action: Amara-Nayaka Commander stands in ornate Vijayanagara armor on a rocky rise, sword lowered but firm, behind him hazy outlines of fort walls, temples and distant cavalry standards under a glowing Deccan sky.

dialogue.text: In the age of Vijayanagara, the king’s will rode on many saddles, carried by the amara-nayakas who held the sword and the land together.

i18n.hi: विजयनगर के दौर में, राजा की मरज़ी कई काठी पर सवार थी, अमर-नायकों के सहारे जो तलवार और ज़मीन दोनों संभालते थे।

panels.action: A vast stone-girt plateau of the Vijayanagara capital spreads below: river curling, rocky hills, temple gopurams, palace compounds and training grounds with lines of horsemen and elephant corps, all under dusty afternoon light.

dialogue.text: On the stony heartland of the Tungabhadra, Vijayanagara rose as a bulwark against northern sultanates, its strength not only in walls and temples, but in the men who commanded its armies.

i18n.hi: तुंगभद्रा की पथरीली धरती पर, विजयनगर उत्तर के सुल्तानों के खिलाफ किले की तरह उभरा — उसकी ताकत सिर्फ़ किलों और मंदिरों में नहीं, उन सैनिक सरदारों में भी थी जो उसकी फौजें चलाते थे।

panels.action: Inside a royal hall, Amara-Nayaka Commander stands among other commanders of varied dress—Telugu, Tamil, Kannada, one in a Muslim-style turban—facing an unseen throne, each holding a palm-leaf grant rolled in their hands.

dialogue.text: They were called amara-nayakas, military chiefs drawn from many tongues and faiths, bound to the Raya by grants of revenue and the duty to bring armed men when the drum was beaten.

i18n.hi: उन्हें अमर-नायक कहा गया — कई जातियों और मजहबों से आए फौजी सरदार, जिन्हें राय ने लगान की जागीरें दीं, और बदले में ढोल बजते ही सशस्त्र सिपाही भेजने की जिम्मेदारी।

panels.action: Close to a stone pillar, Amara-Nayaka Commander studies a carved inscription of a royal grant, finger resting on lines that list villages, irrigation tanks and obligations, a scribe and priest standing respectfully nearby.

dialogue.text: In chiseled stone and on fragile palm leaves, royal grants named the villages and fields entrusted to each nayaka, and the tribute and troops he must return to the capital in exchange.

i18n.hi: तराशी हुई पत्थर की पट्टियों और नाज़ुक ताड़पत्रों पर, दानपत्र हर नायक को सौंपे गाँवों और खेतों के नाम लिखते, और बदले में राजधानी को चढ़ाना पड़ने वाला नज़राना और फ़ौज भी।

panels.action: Amara-Nayaka Commander rides through lush countryside on horseback, flanked by retainers; peasants plough, a small fort rises on a hill, and a local accountant presents tallied copper coins and grain heaps on the roadside.

dialogue.text: Far from the palace, the nayaka’s power lay over fields and tanks and market streets. He collected revenue, kept local order, and turned that income into cavalry, infantry and elephants ready for war.

i18n.hi: महल से दूर, नायक की हुकूमत खेतों, टैंकों और बाज़ार गलियों पर चलती थी। वही लगान लेता, क़ायदा-कानून रखता, और इसी आमदनी को घुड़सवार, पैदल और हाथी-दस्ता बनाकर जंग के लिए तैयार करता।

panels.action: On a dusty parade ground, Amara-Nayaka Commander oversees lines of horsemen practicing swift charges in Sultanate-style saddles, archers under shade, and a small crew testing a matchlock firearm with a foreign gunner guiding.

dialogue.text: The empire’s camps were cosmopolitan schools of war: Deccani cavalry methods, new firearms, and even Portuguese gunners were woven into the host the nayakas must furnish when the Raya called.

i18n.hi: साम्राज्य के डेरों में जंग के रंग-बिरंगे स्कूल बसते थे — दक्खनी घुड़सवारी, नए बारूद वाले हथियार, यहां तक कि पुर्तगाली तोपची भी उस फौज में घुलते जिसे राय की पुकार पर नायकों को भेजना होता।

panels.action: In the Vijayanagara durbar, Amara-Nayaka Commander kneels, presenting a casket of coin and a small model warhorse at the steps of the throne, while courtiers look on and other nayakas wait their turn.

dialogue.text: In return for their amara holdings, the nayakas sent tribute, gifts and men to the capital. Revenue must flow inward; banners must ride outward, or trust between crown and commander frayed.

i18n.hi: अपनी अमर-जागीरों के बदले नायक राजधानी को नज़राना, तोहफ़े और सिपाही भेजते। लगान अंदर की ओर बहना था, और झंडे बाहर की ओर निकलने थे, वरना ताज और तलवार के बीच का भरोसा टूटने लगता।

panels.action: Close on Amara-Nayaka Commander’s face in half-shadow, eyes considering a palm-leaf text laid beside an inscription rubbing that shows the king’s name larger than his own designation as nayaka.

dialogue.text: Centuries later, scholars argued over this balance of power. Was Vijayanagara a segmented realm ruled through many strong local swords, or a centre that held its chiefs in a tighter royal grip?

i18n.hi: सदियों बाद विद्वानों ने इसी ताकत के तालमेल पर बहस की — क्या विजयनगर कई मजबूत तलवारों से चला बिखरा राज्य था, या एक ऐसा केन्द्र जहाँ से राजा नायकों को क़ाबू में रखता रहा?

panels.action: A stylized panel shows the capital on one side with the Raya enthroned, and on the other side Amara-Nayaka Commander in his provincial fort; between them, a chain of small temple-towns and tax stations linking centre and edge.

dialogue.text: Inscriptions show both strands. Nayakas claimed authority near their forts, yet the king’s name still crowned their grants. Power was shared, layered, negotiated across roads and rivers.

i18n.hi: शिलालेख दोनों रंग दिखाते हैं — नायक अपने क़िले के आस-पास ताकत जताते हैं, पर फ़रमानों पर ताज का नाम ही सबसे ऊपर चमकता है। ताकत बंटी हुई, परतों में, सड़कों और नदियों के पार बात-चीत से तय होती दिखती है।

panels.action: Inside a provincial audience hall, Amara-Nayaka Commander hears village headmen and temple officers, while his steward lays out accounts and a soldier waits with a summons scroll bearing the royal seal.

dialogue.text: “The Raya calls. We must ride with full strength, or lose his trust.”

i18n.hi: "राय बुला रहा है। पूरी ताकत से निकलना होगा, नहीं तो उसका भरोसा खो देंगे।"

panels.action: At pale dawn, the nayaka’s contingent marches: horsemen in files, infantry with spears, a few elephants lumbering, a cart carrying gunpowder barrels—Amara-Nayaka Commander rides at the head, looking toward distant hills.

dialogue.text: When war flared against Bahmani successors or Deccan sultanates, the Raya did not summon an imagined million. He called his nayakas, each bringing what his lands and purse could truly sustain.

i18n.hi: जब बहमनी वारिसों या दक्खनी सुल्तानों से जंग भड़की, तो राय ने कोई कल्पित लाखों की फ़ौज नहीं पुकारी; उसने अपने नायकों को बुलाया — हर एक उतने ही सिपाही लाया जितने उसकी ज़मीन और ख़ज़ाना सच में झेल सकते थे।

panels.action: On a hazy battlefield, lines of cavalry clash, elephants push forward, and distant smoke rises from artillery; Amara-Nayaka Commander signals with his sword, keeping his flank steady amid dust and confusion.

dialogue.text: In such storms, nayakas were more than tax-men. They were captains of wings and flanks, holding lines and making choices that could cost them land, honour, or the Raya’s favor.

i18n.hi: ऐसे तूफ़ानों में नायक सिर्फ़ लगान-वसूलने वाले नहीं रहे; वे पंखों और मोर्चों के सरदार बने, मोर्चे थामे रहे और ऐसे फ़ैसले किए जिनकी कीमत ज़मीन, इज़्ज़त, या राय की नज़र में गिरावट हो सकती थी।

panels.action: The battlefield after combat: abandoned tents, broken standards, tired soldiers resting; Amara-Nayaka Commander stands apart, looking toward where the capital’s direction lies, aware that victory and loss both change reputations.

dialogue.text: A successful campaign could bring a fresh amara assignment, more villages to govern, more men to command. Failure could invite suspicion, reassignment, or a slow pruning of a nayaka’s reach.

i18n.hi: कामयाब मुहिम एक नई अमर-जागीर ला सकती थी — नए गाँव, नई रियासत, और ज़्यादा सिपाहियों पर हुक्म। नाकामी शक, तबादला, या नायक की पहुँच को धीरे-धीरे काट देने का सबब बन सकती थी।

panels.action: Inside a provincial fort after some years, Amara-Nayaka Commander presides over a grand festival; local poets sing his praises, artisans present gifts, while a small portrait of the distant Raya hangs above.

dialogue.text: Over time, some nayakas turned their assignments into almost princely seats. They built temples, held festivals, and had ballads sung in their names, even as they still acknowledged the Raya above them.

i18n.hi: समय के साथ कुछ नायकों ने अपनी जागीरों को लगभग रजवाड़ों में बदल दिया। उन्होंने मंदिर बनाए, मेले करवाए, और अपने नाम के वीर-गीत गवाए — फिर भी ऊपर बैठे राय को मानते रहे।

panels.action: A stark wide shows Vijayanagara’s skyline darkened: temples intact but royal quarters smoking faintly; in the distance the Tungabhadra valley lies silent, signaling the fall of the capital after a crushing defeat.

dialogue.text: When the great defeat at Talikota broke Vijayanagara’s heartland and the capital fell, the centre that had bound the nayakas together was shaken. The royal drum at Hampi went quiet.

i18n.hi: जब तालिकोट की बड़ी हार ने विजयनगर का दिल तोड़ दिया और राजधानी ढह गई, तो वही केन्द्र हिल गया जो नायकों को एक सूत में बांधे था। हम्पी का शाही नगाड़ा ख़ामोश हो गया।

panels.action: Close on Amara-Nayaka Commander’s face as he gazes from his provincial fort rampart, the news of the capital’s fall heavy, yet behind him retainers, scribes and soldiers still look to him for direction.

dialogue.text: “The throne has fallen, but the land and men remain. What now?”

i18n.hi: "तख़्त तो गिर गया, पर ज़मीन और आदमी तो हैं… अब क्या?"

panels.action: A sweeping montage: different fortified towns—Madurai with its temple towers, Tanjore’s tall gopuram, Ikkeri in the west, Gingee’s hill forts—each with a nayaka-like figure on the ramparts, echoing Amara-Nayaka Commander’s stance.

dialogue.text: From these dispersed swords grew new kingdoms. Madurai, Tanjore, Ikkeri, Gingee and others became successor states, ruled by former nayakas who now held crowns over the lands they once served.

i18n.hi: इन्हीं बिखरे हुए तलवारों से नए राज उगे — मदुरै, तंजौर, इक्केरी, जिंजी और दूसरे — जहां पहले के नायक अब उन्हीं ज़मीनों पर ताज पहनकर राज करने लगे जिनकी कभी वे सेवा करते थे।

panels.action: Extreme close-up on a later-period stone inscription naming a Nayaka ruler, with faint, older Vijayanagara grant-rubbing beside it; Amara-Nayaka Commander’s hand hovers over both in a symbolic gesture linking past and future.

dialogue.text: The record in stone shows continuity and change. Titles shifted, loyalties were rewritten, but the system of revenue, military duty, and local command carried Vijayanagara’s imprint into a new political map.

i18n.hi: पत्थर की लिखावटें निरंतरता और बद्लाव दोनों दिखाती हैं — खिताब बदले, वफादारियाँ दोबारा लिखी गईं, मगर लगान, फौजी फ़र्ज़ और स्थानीय हुकूमत का ढांचा विजयनगर की छाप लेकर ही नए सियासी नक्शे पर चला।

panels.action: Amara-Nayaka Commander stands silhouetted against a wide South Indian landscape of fields, rivers and temple spires, his sword sheathed, his hand resting on a scroll of grants as the sun lowers on the horizon.

dialogue.text: The amara-nayakas remind us that delegated power must be bound by duty. When sword and land are given into many hands, their loyalty can uphold a realm—or, freed of the centre, reshape it entirely.

i18n.hi: अमर-नायक हमें याद दिलाते हैं कि बाँटी हुई ताकत को फ़र्ज़ की रस्सी से बांधना पड़ता है — जब तलवार और ज़मीन कई हाथों में बाँटी जाती है, तो उनकी वफ़ादारी एक राज्य को संभाल भी सकती है… या केन्द्र से आज़ाद होकर उसे बिल्कुल नया रूप भी दे सकती है।

title_i18n.en: The Nayakas Who Held the Sword

subtitle_i18n.en: How the Sword-Bearing Nayakas Carried, Shared, and Remade the Power of Vijayanagara
subtitle_i18n.hi: कैसे तलवारधारी नायकों ने विजयनगर की ताकत को उठाया, बांटा और नया रुप दिया
